title: Multifunctional Barophotonic Control of Resonators and Metasurfaces
authors: Ping-Chun Chen, Mashnoon Alam Sakib, Mariia Stepanova, Melika Momenzadeh, Maxim R. Shcherbakov
abstract: Actively tunable nanophotonic platforms that control light-matter interactions enable reconfigurable optical systems and programmable photonic integrated circuits. Hydrostatic pressure provides a noninvasive and material-agnostic mechanism for modulating the refractive index and resonance conditions without introducing free carriers or structural damage. Here, we demonstrate multiple pressure-dependent functionalities in silicon nitride nanostructures, including resonance tuning, refractive index modulation, and polarization state conversion. Applying a pressure of up to 5 GPa, we observe a Fabry-Pérot resonance shift of up to 30 nm and a relative refractive index decrease of up to 4%. Based on the results, we design and examine, to the best of our knowledge, the first extreme-pressure-tunable, polarization-converting metasurface, which tunes the ellipticity and orientation angle of the output light. These findings establish pressure-controllable silicon nitride as a viable platform for reconfigurable photonics and extreme-environment nanophotonic systems, including deep-ocean exploration, planetary interiors, and space applications.
summary: This paper demonstrates a method to control the optical properties of silicon nitride nanostructures using extreme hydrostatic pressure. By applying up to 5 GPa, the researchers achieved significant tuning of resonance wavelengths and polarization states, providing a platform for reconfigurable photonics in extreme environments.
main_problem: The need for actively tunable nanophotonic platforms to enable reconfigurable optical systems and the challenge of achieving simultaneous pressure-induced control over multiple functionalities like resonance and polarization.
main_result: Demonstrated that hydrostatic pressure up to 5 GPa can induce a 30 nm blueshift in SiN resonances and a 4% refractive index decrease, enabling the first extreme-pressure-tunable, polarization-converting metasurface.
method: High-pressure experiments using a Diamond Anvil Cell (DAC) with ruby fluorescence for pressure calibration, combined with FDTD simulations and Stokes parameter reconstruction for polarization analysis.
model_or_system: Amorphous silicon nitride (SiN) nanostructures, specifically metal-coated Fabry-Perot resonators and a grating metasurface, subjected to hydrostatic pressure in a methanol:ethanol:water mixture.
key_observables: Transmittance spectra, resonance wavelength shift, refractive index modulation, polarization orientation angle (psi), and ellipticity (chi).
important_parameters: Pressure up to 5 GPa, refractive index change (dn/dP = -1.5e-2 GPa^-1), and resonance blueshift of up to 30 nm.
main_assumptions: The pressure medium is assumed to maintain hydrostatic conditions; the resonance shift is assumed to be dominated by refractive index changes rather than thickness variations.
figures_summary: Fig 1: DAC setup and SiN FP resonator structure with simulation/experiment comparison. Fig 2: Pressure-dependent resonance blueshift and extracted refractive index decrease. Fig 3: Metasurface configuration, SEM images, and electric field simulations. Fig 4: Polarization state tunability (orientation and ellipticity).
paper_structure: Introduction to tunable photonics, experimental setup description (DAC and SiN structures), results for Fabry-Perot resonance tuning, results for metasurface polarization control, and discussion of the physical mechanism and applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: SiN Fabry–P´erot resonators under high pressure. a, Schematic side view of the high-pressure diamond anvil cell setup, where the hole in the gasket between the diamonds forms the high-pressure chamber. The Fabry–P´erot resonator sample was illuminated by white light, while ruby was excited by a 532 nm laser and functions as a pressure indicator. By forcing the diamonds inward, pressure is applied to the sample through the pressure medium. The structure of the Fabry–P´erot resonator is illustrated in the inset, showing a l = 100 nm thick SiN sample coated with t = 30 nm gold on both sides and exposed to the white light source. The magnitude of the resonator electric field along the...

Figure 2: Refractive index tunability of SiN under pressure. a, Peak position of the first- order mode of the FP resonance shifts as a function of pressure. The squares, triangles, and circles represent different samples, and the solid lines are linear fits. For all samples, the peak positions exhibit blueshifts of up to 30 nm with increasing pressure up to 5 GPa. b, The refractive index of SiN, n(P), as extracted using Eq. (1). A decrease of 4% in n(P) under 5 GPa of applied pressure is consistent across different samples.

Figure 3: The configuration and the resonance tunability of a multifunctional barophotonic metasurface. a, The configuration of the metasurface placed in the DAC setup along with the optical components used for analyzing polarization states. The polarizer placed at θ = 45◦

Figure 4: Polarization state tunability of the barophotonic metasurface. a-b, The orientation ψ and ellipticity χ of light transmitted through metasurface (‘MS’, solid lines) and without metasurface (‘No MS’, the dashed lines) at ambient pressure and at 1.32 GPa, respectively. The inset shows the measured ‘MS’ and ‘No MS’ regions in the DAC. c-d, Experimental differentials ψMS −ψNoMS and χMS −χNoMS as functions of wavelength and pressure. e-f, Simulated ψ and χ as functions of wavelength and pressure.
